q1.question: When you write a caption, what does your opening line usually look like — and what do you want the reader to do after reading it?
options: We start with a question or bold claim, but clicks and saves stay low
We describe what the post is about, then add a CTA at the end

Something else
q1.insight: Posts with vague hooks lose 80% of readers in the first 8 words.
q1.diagnostic_intent: Identify whether the lead-generation gap lives in hook quality, CTA clarity, or structural inconsistency — probing P1, P2, and O4 simultaneously.
q1.cumulative_insight: User wants social media captions and hooks to drive lead generation in a content/social media context. This signals the core gap is likely between posting volume and pipeline impact — they're probably publishing but not converting followers into leads. Root cause is most likely in hook vagueness (P1), missing audience-pain framing (P2), or a CTA that optimizes for likes rather than intent (P7). No user responses yet — hypothesis to be confirmed.

Their goal is to use AI to create scalable product visuals that drive leads — not just brand awareness. This signals they care about performance metrics (CTR, CVR, cost-per-lead), not just aesthetics. Key risk areas to probe: whether their input assets are strong enough to anchor AI generation (P1/P2), whether their creatives are built around a clear message and placement spec (P4/P5), and whether they have any feedback loop to know what's working (S4/S7).

We ask manually, so it's inconsistent and depends on who remembers
We don't really have a review collection process yet
Something else
q1.insight: Businesses lose 68% of review volume by asking at the wrong moment.
q1.diagnostic_intent: Probing P1 + P4: timing logic and experience-based segmentation — the two highest-leverage levers for review volume and rating skew
q1.cumulative_insight: User is in Customer Success & Reputation, focused on improving retention and reducing churn. Their goal ties reviews directly to revenue — poor reputation signals are likely accelerating churn, not just hurting acquisition. The core diagnostic question is whether their review process is proactive and segmented, or reactive and undifferentiated. No user responses yet — initial hypothesis: unsegmented outreach is creating rating skew and missing the operational feedback loop that would actually reduce churn.

Root cause hypothesis is shifting toward a broken review funnel that lacks both upstream triage and downstream response governance. Next diagnostic priority: determine whether negative reviews are being caught and resolved quickly, or sitting unanswered and eroding conversion.

Everything is in one sheet — data, calcu.acknowledgment: A single mixed sheet is the #1 structural fragility pattern — one column shift can silently break every downstream calculation.

The time drain is not just manual work — it is invisible rework triggered by automation that appears to be running but is producing stale or corrupted output. This question will determine whether breakage manifests as loud failures (fixable with monitoring) or silent corruption (requires architectural separation first).

They come back for revisions — tone, sco.cumulative_insight: The core time drain is confirmed at two stages: (1) Production — user starts every document from scratch with no templates or reusable structure, forcing full cognitive load each time. (2) Post-production — documents come back for revisions around tone, scope, or missing details, creating a rework loop. This rules out approval process delays as the primary bottleneck. Root cause hypothesis is now tightening: the absence of a structured writing system (templates with pre-filled scope sections, tone guidelines, CTA prompts) means every document is both slow to produce AND likely to miss reader expectations. Still unknown: whether the revision trigger is primarily a structural gap (missing sections like scope/assumptions/next steps), a tone-calibration gap, or a framing mismatch — this will determine whether the fix is a template system, an audience-briefing step, or a review checklist.

Proposals specifically don't convert — w.acknowledgment: Proposals that don't convert are often a structural problem, not a pricing one — the issue usually lives inside the document itself.
Proposals specifically don't convert — w.cumulative_insight: Two confirmed root causes are now in view. First, the blank-page problem: no templates or reusable structure means every document starts from zero, creating a heavy cognitive load and time drain at the production stage. Second, a conversion problem specific to proposals: documents are being sent but rarely winning. This points to a downstream structural failure — likely missing scope clarity, absent proof points, or an executive summary that doesn't compel action. The working hypothesis is that solving both requires a writing system (templates + reusable blocks) AND a proposal operating model (discovery inputs → structured outline → scope/assumptions → proof → QA → send). Approval delays and revision loops remain unconfirmed as significant pain points.

Getting the tone and framing right — too.question: When you're rewriting — what's the core trigger? Is it that the first draft sounds too formal/robotic, too casual, or that you're unsure how the reader will interpret the framing?
options: Too formal or stiff — doesn't sound like me
Too casual — worried it won't land with the audience
I can't tell how the reader will react, so I keep second-guessing
The structure feels off — I know what to say but not how to sequence it
Getting the tone and framing right — too.insight: Docs without tone templates average 3.2 rewrites before sign-off.
Getting the tone and framing right — too.acknowledgment: Rewrites are the hidden tax — most people don't realize revision loops, not drafting, consume 60–70% of total writing time.
Getting the tone and framing right — too.cumulative_insight: User's primary time drain is rewrites, not blank-page paralysis or approval delays. They're getting drafts out but cycling through multiple rounds before feeling confident enough to send. This points away from a volume or process problem and toward a quality calibration issue — likely missing a reliable 'voice and framing' reference that tells them what 'good' looks like for their context. Root cause hypothesis: no internalized tone standard or audience model, so every draft is judged against a moving target. Approval bottlenecks and template gaps are lower probability at this stage.

Too casual — worried it won't land with.cumulative_insight: User's core time drain is rewrites driven by tone miscalibration — drafts come out too casual and they lack confidence they'll land with the intended audience. The structure feels off — I know what to.cumulative_insight: User's core time drain is rewrites, not blank-page paralysis or approval delays. This is a structural calibration gap, not a confidence or vocabulary gap. Hypothesis: no reusable document skeleton that mirrors how the target reader makes decisions (action first, evidence second). It doesn't tell me what to do — just what happened, no recommendations

q1.insight: 80% of summaries stall decisions — they miss the 3–7 numbers that actually matter.
q1.diagnostic_intent: Identify the primary failure mode in current summary output — whether the gap is in content completeness, action-orientation, or traceability/trust
q1.cumulative_insight: User wants to save time in personal and team productivity by summarizing PDFs and long documents. This signals a high-volume reading burden — likely multiple document types (reports, research, contracts) flowing to different audiences. The core pain is time lost, but the real risk is decision delay or bad decisions made on incomplete summaries. Root cause is unknown — could be output quality, audience mismatch, or triage/prioritization of which docs get summarized at all.
